other_observers: None really, though Andy Anderson stopped as he saw me photographing and after I pointed it out to him he said there are scads of those in Florida. He is a fisherman with a winter home in Florida. (I told you this was an upscale area.); I am the original, and really only. I showed pictures of the bird today to life-long residents who have never seen such a bird. In fairness, however, they don't know a house finch from a house sparrow.
duration: 5 to 10 minutes. After I got what I thought were the best shots I could get, I left.
specific_location: Country Club Hills, an upscale housing area a mile west of Sterling.
description: The purple was not as deep as the painting in Sibley's, my only other knowledge of this bird. As noted above, its plumage was not as pristine as I might have expected. Beaten a little but not badly. The flecks of gray feathers in the two blues and a bit on the back make me wonder if it could be molting or still just getting its seasonal, adult plumage. It's back was also not as green as in Sibley's nor did it reach as high up on the neck.Behaviors: Since I have never seen one I instantly knew it was a rarity and after quickly discounting that it was some coot in the rushes, I knew what it was from pictures--although my mind said "galliute."  Huge feet, it walked on floating vegetation, at times clinging clumsily to dead branches on a bush that has died in the recently-formed pond.Call: I heard none.Plumage: Slightly ragged
